author: @lukasvandermerwe929

author: @franzschmidt6199
text: Very cute, Rob, you brought back memories. The reason you could keep on pumping without slowing it down was that the knob at the top of the toy was freely spinning on the shaft. Too bad you did not let it hum for us. That would have completed my joy of seeing it again. Congrats on realizing that this would be useful to drive a generator with! For that you are getting my thumbs up.

author: @elpresidente4441
text: Hi Robert. Have you ever heard about Carbon-cement-supercapacitors? There is a research going on at MIT to mix cement with carbon black to make a highly conductive concrete in order to build supercapacitors with a huge capacity. It seems to be a very interesting concept for energy storage in the future. I'm not allowed to put a direct link in here but there is an official paper in the PNAS-Magazine which describes this concept in detail, written by James C. Weaver, Franz-Josef Ulm and some others. It would be great if you could find the time to check this out. You may want to share your thoughts about it in a future video? I just love your channel and highly appreaciate your interesting work.

Oooohhhh, i remember playing with those as a kid! A wonderful toy and amazing mechanism :)

author: @thesustainabilitysoonerchannel
text: 👏👏👏Oh, Robert!!! That's me clapping with glee, just like I did as a child on Christmas Day when Santa brought me a humming top!  There were no cell phones to charge back then.  Nonetheless, hours of joyous play competing with my friends and myself to make it spin faster and longer built-up our baby biceps.  Thx for transforming those fond memories 💓into yet another ingenious, inexpensive power generation idea...👍😎👍

author: @mitchell-paris
text:
Question: Why do you put your serpentine coil on the side of the part that is facing away from the magnets? Wouldn't keeping the coil closer generate more power?
I'm a newer watcher for the last few months and I haven't heard an explanation about this yet, so if there is a video talking about it please forgive me.
Thank you for these videos which are slowly making me want to get into 3D printing and energy generation!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it s facing the magnets mate the magnets are arranged on the face of the inner spinning disk

replies.author: @mikebond6328
replies.text: I think he means, why not put the coil on the inside of the outer ring so there’s no plastic between the magnets and the coil. I’m sure there’s a good reason it wasn’t done that way.

This is so crazy!  
Just yesterday I was thinking about these exact toys and I was thinking they are perfect for Rob's tinkering..   woke up today and here is the video!   ...Eeerie.

author: @NiteLynr
text:
Fantastic video as always. Loving the fact that you added decorative details to this one; no reason you can't have function /and/ form!
I do have a couple of questions though.
1) I see that it wobbles quite a bit at speed. would it be possible to add a bearing to the lower end of the push rod to keep everything smooth? I feel that that might improve the output duration a bit.
2) Been meaning to ask this for months but where did you get your green coat from? I love my long coats and that one is magnificent looking.
Keep up the great work. It's always a pleasure to see you videos appearing in my feed as your enthusiasm is so refreshing after a long day at work.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
If one tied a mechanism like that to the branches of a tree where the swaying motion would move it, it would almost never stop moving in some areas.

Admittedly, during a severe storm, it might end up being damaged, but if you used the right arrangement of cables and pulleys, it might be fine to put the device inside a box at ground level, and have cables pulling up and down [or left and right?] through holes in a protective box to make the spiral drive actuate.

replies.author: @hoog111
replies.text: You could use bungees to stabilise excess movement

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
My grandma had a top like that and I played with it now and then, and I knew it had ball bearings in it that made it a one-way-clutch.

Luckily for whatever toy collector who may have ever obtained it decades later, I never dismantled it to see how it worked.
